## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

5e677ec6  ₢A-AAv    Rewrite Windows-platform obliterate as four-namespace split-atom sequence in zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces. Each PowerShell call is one atom — either a probe that emits raw text (Get-LocalUser, Test-Path on profile/Cygwin paths, wsl.exe --list, wsl.exe bash -c 'getent passwd ... 2>/dev/null || true') or one destructive operation (Remove-LocalUser, Remove-Item, wsl.exe userdel -r). All conditional logic moves bash-side via test/case + `|| buc_die` per BCG; PowerShell carries no `if` statements. Drops the brittle one-liner that kept tripping PowerShell's $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' native-command stderr-to-terminating-error escalation regardless of redirect form (verified failures with 2>&1 | Out-Null, *>$null, and 2>$null | Out-Null all at the same column-arrow position on net.exe absent-state stderr). Three of four namespace probes now use only PS cmdlets so no native-command escalation surface exists; only the wsl.exe getent probe stays in native-command territory and pushes the absent-tolerance into bash inside wsl via `|| true`. WSL user probe argument uses a PowerShell single-quoted string with '' escapes (rather than embedded double quotes) so cmd.exe's outer powershell -Command "..." tokenization doesn't break on inner quote pairs (the previous bash -c "..." form fragmented at cmd.exe parse time, surfacing as 'true"; if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0)... is not recognized as an internal or external command'). Two new kindle constants ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT/STDERR (reused across the up-to-eight per-namespace calls; last call's content preserved at die-time, referenced in buc_die paths). Per-sub-step buc_step lines ([SAM]/[Profile]/[Cygwin]/[WSL]) localize failures to the specific namespace. Verified end-to-end progression on bujn-winpc: obliterate ran clean across all four namespaces (all absent on first run, expected); steps 3 (create) and 4 (place trust at /mnt/c/Users/bujuw_user/.ssh/authorized_keys — path message now correct per the earlier per-letter z_authkeys_dir fix) reported success; step 5 (plant key) failed inside WSL with `install: invalid user 'bujuw_user'`. Step-5 failure is unrelated to the obliterate work — pre-existing step-3 issue surfaced now that fenestrate/garrison ran end-to-end for the first time. Two hypotheses: (A) wsl.exe swallowed step-3 useradd's non-zero exit, masking the failure from set -e in the heredoc; (B) user created but lost between step 3 and step 5. Diagnostic probe attempted via tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh bujn-winpc 'wsl.exe --user root cat /etc/passwd' but rocket became SSH-unreachable (Operation timed out on connect — not refused, not auth failure — host went away between the garrison run and probe).   Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.

  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

message.content:
Lets prepare the work for this but keep it in the chat so as not to disrupt the parallel chat which is building.

Lets talk through the precise approach to nopasswd.   I'm waffling on following questions:
1) qualified NOPASSWD: I _think_ we can designate just some applications for nopassword.  Is this good for us?
2) Should we design a superuser identity specifically for privileged operation with a highly honed nopasswd list?
3) I recall sometimes seeing edits in a sudo file versus 'sudoers' snippets being placed at special locations.  What is more standard?

content.text:
Good design questions to settle before code. My read on each:

**Q1 — Qualified NOPASSWD: yes, strongly recommend.**

sudoers supports per-command qualification:

```
user ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/userdel, /usr/sbin/useradd, ...
```

The blast radius shrinks from "any privileged operation" to exactly the verbs garrison invokes. Auditing the operator's machine for "what RBM can do without a password" becomes a single grep instead of a trust judgment. The cost is a longer paste — fine, the snippet is static.

Walking the current call sites, the qualified set is:

- **Linux:** `userdel`, `useradd`, `passwd`, `rm`, `mkdir`, `chmod`, `tee`, `chown`, `install`
- **Mac:** `sysadminctl`, `dscl`, `rm`, `mkdir`, `chmod`, `tee`, `chown`, `install`

One subtle interaction with the preflight probe: `sudo -n true` needs `/usr/bin/true` in the list as a witness, or the probe needs to invoke an actual operational command (e.g., `sudo -n /usr/sbin/useradd -V`). I'd include `/usr/bin/true` as an explicit witness — keeps the probe trivial and decouples it from operational command paths that drift across distros (`/sbin` vs `/usr/sbin`).

**Q2 — Dedicated superuser identity: don't bother.**

The argument for it (defense in depth — leak of garrison key only enables user provisioning) is real but weakened by qualified NOPASSWD already constraining what the existing admin identity can do without a password. The cost (two identities, two keys, fenestrate complexity multiplied) outpaces the marginal security gain.

The privileged identity is purpose-built for ops infrastructure work, not daily admin — adding a second identity within that already-narrow scope is duplication. If the threat model later expands (shared admin nodes, audit trail requirements), introducing a `BURP_GARRISON_USER` then is an additive change. Defer until needed.

One platform nuance worth knowing: macOS `sysadminctl` requires admin-group membership, not just sudoers. So `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` on Mac has to be in the `admin` group regardless of which identity strategy you pick — the qualified NOPASSWD just removes the password prompt on top.

**Q3 — `/etc/sudoers.d/` drop-in, not direct `/etc/sudoers` edit.**

Strongly the modern standard. Reasons:

- **Atomic install/remove.** One file, owned root, mode 0440. Removing RBM's NOPASSWD is `rm /etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<user>`. Direct edits leave you grepping `/etc/sudoers` for what to delete.
- **Failure isolation.** A syntax error in `/etc/sudoers` itself locks you out of sudo entirely (this is why `visudo` exists). A syntax error in a drop-in fragment is silently skipped per-file (or refused if validated with `visudo -cf` first), and the master file keeps working.
- **Convention.** Distros and packages overwhelmingly ship sudoers fragments via `/etc/sudoers.d/`; operators recognize it. Hand-editing `/etc/sudoers` reads as legacy.
- **Both platforms support it.** macOS has `/etc/sudoers.d/` since well before any version we'd target.

Filename rule that bites people: drop-in files can't contain dots and can't end in `~`. `rb-garrison-<user>` (no dots) is safe.

**Recommended snippet shape** (the visudo line in the diagnostic):

```
# Save as /etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER>
Cmnd_Alias RB_GARRISON = \
    /usr/sbin/userdel, /usr/sbin/useradd, /usr/bin/passwd, \
    /usr/bin/install, /usr/bin/mkdir, /usr/bin/tee, \
    /usr/bin/chmod, /usr/bin/chown, /bin/rm, /usr/bin/true
<BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: RB_GARRISON
```

Operator install command (single paste):

```
sudo visudo -cf <(echo '...') && \
  echo '...' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<user> > /dev/null && \
  sudo chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<user>
```

A heredoc form is friendlier; the diagnostic builder can emit either.

---

**Net design summary:**

1. Qualified NOPASSWD with a curated command list per platform (Linux set ≠ Mac set).
2. Reuse `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`; no new identity.
3. Drop-in at `/etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<user>` (mode 0440), validated by `visudo -cf`.
4. Preflight probes `sudo -n true`; `/usr/bin/true` is in the NOPASSWD list as the witness.

If you agree with that direction, the docket as written is mostly fine but needs two refinements:

- Replace "emits a copy-paste visudo line" with "emits a copy-paste `/etc/sudoers.d/` drop-in fragment with `Cmnd_Alias`-qualified NOPASSWD."
- Note the per-platform command-set divergence (Linux vs Mac) — the helper has two cases, not one.

Does the recommended direction match what you want? Anything you'd push back on?

message.content: I'm musing that our garrison operation ought to first check for proper permissions.  If the remote system is macos, then garrison should failfast if macOS sysadminctl user group membership is not correct.  Linux case is weirder, but I suspect it is sensible for us to write into a temp file beforehand the synthesized sudoer snippet and fail if it is not present verbatim in the target filesystem.  What is the impact of multiple orthogonal entries?  Can a user have more NOPASSWD if the main sudoer definition file does not qualify their allowed apps?

content.text:
**On the merge question first** (because it informs everything else):

sudoers rules are **purely additive across files**. The parser reads `/etc/sudoers`, then walks `@includedir /etc/sudoers.d/` lexically; every matching rule contributes, and **last-match-wins per (user, host, runas, command) tuple**. A drop-in saying `user ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/userdel` does not require — and is not blocked by — anything in the master file. The master can keep its strict `user ALL=(ALL) ALL` posture; for `userdel` specifically, the drop-in rule matches later and its NOPASSWD tag wins. For every other command, only the master rule matches → password required, unchanged.

So our drop-in is genuinely surgical: it carves out NOPASSWD for exactly our garrison command set without touching any other policy on the box. This is the cleanest case for the drop-in approach.

**On the macOS group check: yes, do it.**

`sysadminctl` and `dscl` both refuse non-admin invokers regardless of NOPASSWD. The probe is read-only and doesn't itself need sudo:

```
dseditgroup -o checkmember -m "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}" admin
```

Returns 0 if member, non-0 otherwise. Pair it with the sudo probe — both must pass. The diagnostic then has two failure modes (group missing → "add to admin group"; sudo missing → "drop in this snippet").

**On the Linux verbatim-file-presence approach:**

I'd push back gently. The behavioral question we actually care about is *"will `sudo -n` succeed for these commands?"*, not *"does this file exist verbatim?"* — and the latter is more brittle:

- Reading `/etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<user>` itself needs root, so you'd need NOPASSWD on `cat` of that file → recursive bootstrap.
- Defaults-line drift, harmless whitespace, or `Cmnd_Alias` reorganization would all fail a verbatim check while leaving behavior intact.
- It misses the case where the operator's site already has broader NOPASSWD configured upstream — perfectly fine for us, but our check would refuse it.

The cleaner test is `sudo -nl`, which lists what the user can sudo without password. It returns parseable output even on non-elevated sessions, and crucially it queries *effective* permissions across all sudoers sources:

```
sudo -nl 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'NOPASSWD.*<cmd>'
```

Run it once, parse for each expected command in the platform's set. If any are missing, emit the diagnostic. This is honest about what we depend on, accommodates operators who installed the rules differently than our default snippet suggests, and avoids the chicken-and-egg of reading the sudoers file itself.

**Refined preflight shape:**

| Platform | Check 1 | Check 2 |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Linux | — | `sudo -nl` parsed for expected command set |
| Mac | `dseditgroup -o checkmember -m USER admin` | `sudo -nl` parsed for expected command set |

Diagnostic on failure names which check failed and emits the corresponding fix (admin-group `dseditgroup -o edit` for Mac group; drop-in fragment for sudo). Both diagnostics name `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` and the platform.

Does the `sudo -nl`-parsing approach sit right with you, or do you want to keep verbatim-file-check as a stricter belt-and-suspenders option on top?

message.content: I actually do like the sudo -nl approach; one line per command though, probably wrap in a bash function.  Is that clear as an approach?  I still want a temp file prepared with the snippet so that on fail, there is a clear recommendation.  In fact, we perhaps ought to go ahead and copy that to the remote's filesystem

content.text:
Yes, clear — and per-command via exit code is even cleaner than parsing list output.

**Sudo probe:** `sudo -l <command>` (with `-n`) is its own per-command query — sudo evaluates whether that specific command is permitted and exits 0/1 accordingly. No output parsing required. Wrapped:

```
zbujb_garrison_b_preflight_probe_sudo() {
  local z_cmd="${1:?cmd required}"
  zbujb_admin_exec b "sudo -ln '${z_cmd}' </dev/null >/dev/null 2>&1"
}
```

Caller iterates the platform's expected command list and calls this helper once per command. On the first failure, jump to the diagnostic. With this shape, `/usr/bin/true` drops out of the list — every probed command is a real garrison verb, no synthetic witness needed.

**Staging the snippet to remote: yes, do it, but only on failure.**

The happy path stays fast (no extra ssh round-trip when everything's already configured). On the first probe miss:

1. Render the platform-appropriate snippet locally to a controller-side temp file.
2. `scp` it to the remote at `~/.cache/rb-garrison/<BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER>.sudoers`. `~/.cache/` over `/tmp/` because /tmp is cleared on reboot and tmpwatch — the operator might let the diagnostic sit overnight before acting.
3. Run `visudo -cf ~/.cache/rb-garrison/...` over admin SSH to validate syntax remotely (no sudo needed; visudo's `-cf` only reads).
4. `buc_die` with a copy-paste-safe install line:

```
sudo install -m 0440 -o root -g root \
    ~/.cache/rb-garrison/<user>.sudoers \
    /etc/sudoers.d/rb-garrison-<user>
```

Operator types password once, runs that one line, garrison reruns clean.

Two reasons this is materially better than emitting the snippet in the diagnostic and asking the operator to paste it themselves:

- **Already validated on their machine.** `visudo -cf` confirmed it parses against their sudo's grammar version. No surprise rejection at install-time.
- **Single-line install.** No multi-line heredoc paste hazards (zsh/bash quoting differences, terminal mangling), no chance of typo.

**One subtlety to call out:** the staged file's content must match what we expect *exactly* if we want the install path to be deterministic. So the snippet renderer is pure-content — same input, same output, byte-for-byte. That keeps the file useful as a stable artifact across re-runs (operator can keep the path bookmarked).

**Structure I'm picturing:**

| Helper | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight` | Top entry; dispatches checks per platform |
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight_probe_sudo` | One-command sudo probe (above) |
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight_expected_commands` | Emits the platform's command list (linux ≠ mac) |
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight_render_snippet` | Pure renderer: platform → snippet content |
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight_stage_and_die` | scp + visudo -cf + buc_die with install line |
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight_die_admin_group` | Mac admin-group failure path (no staging) |

Six helpers feels right for the operation's structure — each one purpose, no incidental sharing. The expected-commands and render-snippet pair could be platform-tagged constants instead of helpers, but a function lets the renderer interpolate `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` cleanly.

content.text:
Legitimate wince. The name is long because we're stacking four qualifiers on every helper (`bujb_garrison_b_preflight_*`) — that's the symptom of an abstraction wanting its own seat.

**Choices:**

**(1) Keep inside bujb** — symmetric with existing `zbujb_garrison_w_preflight`. Names stay long. Lowest disruption. Premature-abstraction defense holds: w-preflight is one function, b-preflight is six, "third instance" hasn't materialized yet.

**(2) Trim qualifier inside bujb** — drop "garrison" since the file IS jurisdiction. `zbujb_b_preflight*`. But for symmetry the existing `zbujb_garrison_w_preflight` would need renaming too — and that file is exactly what the parallel chat is editing. Off the table this turn.

**(3) Mint `bujp_preflight.sh`** — preflight gets its own seat. `zbujp_b_*` naming. Cost: new file, new zipper enrollment, sourcing from somewhere, acronym-map update. Future migration of the w-preflight is a small mechanical pace once the parallel chat lands.

**My lean: (3) — earn the seat now.**

The b-preflight at six helpers (probe, expected-commands, render, stage-and-die, admin-group, entry) is substantial enough to warrant minting on its own merits. Stacking it sideways into bujb under stretched naming is the kind of decision that ages into "we should have split this" debt. Better to mint while the cost is low (one new file, one new zipper line) than to extract under load later.

Some good positioning effects:

- **Concept boundary becomes crisp.** Preflight = "precondition check + diagnostic + recovery artifact." That's a coherent shape that recurs (fenestrate's sshd-running probe, potential c-letter discovery probe, etc.). Each new preflight slots into bujp without bloating bujb.
- **bujb stops accreting the preflight branch.** Garrison stays focused on the ceremony itself; preflight becomes a precondition called from garrison's entry.
- **Naming is short by construction.** `zbujp_b_render`, `zbujp_b_probe`, `zbujp_b_stage` — readable without contractions.

**Sketch of the bujp module surface:**

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `bujp_b_preflight` | Public entry (called from garrison) |
| `zbujp_b_probe` | Single-command sudo probe |

message.content: what is the _b_: bash?  Should mac admin group thing be 'b' prefixed?  Should we be discussing moving other preflight concepts from bujb?  Is there a better module split given all our current functions?

content.text:
Good challenge. Each question pulls on a different thread — let me walk them.

**"_b_: bash?"** — Yes, shell-letter b (native bash, vs c=Cygwin, w=WSL). It's an existing organizing dimension because garrison's *ceremony* genuinely differs per shell-letter (different remote shells, different command sets). But for preflight, the dimension is **platform**, not shell — and they only happen to align (linux/mac→b, windows→c|w). Calling preflight by shell-letter is conflating two axes.

**"Should mac admin group be 'b' prefixed?"** — No, you're right to flinch. `dseditgroup` has nothing to do with bash; it's a macOS user-model probe. Lumping it under `_b_` because Mac happens to use the b shell-letter is incidental alignment masquerading as load-bearing structure.

The honest dimension for preflight is platform (and target subsystem on Windows). Renamings drop out:

| Current / proposed | Honest name |
|--------------------|-------------|
| `zbujb_garrison_w_preflight` (existing) | `zbujp_garrison_wsl_preflight` — checks WSL distro present |
| `zbujb_garrison_b_preflight` (proposed) | split into `zbujp_garrison_linux_preflight` + `zbujp_garrison_mac_preflight` |

The Linux helper does sudo-NOPASSWD only. The Mac helper does admin-group + sudo-NOPASSWD. They share sub-helpers (`zbujp_probe_sudo`, `zbujp_render_snippet`, `zbujp_stage_and_die`). The dispatcher (called from garrison entry) is a 3-arm case on `BURN_PLATFORM`.

This is materially cleaner than the previous proposal — each preflight entry is self-contained for its platform, diagnostic flow is platform-honest, and the shared mechanism is shared-by-substance not shared-by-naming-convention.

**"Move other preflight concepts from bujb?"** — Yes, two:

- `zbujb_garrison_w_preflight` (the existing one) → `zbujp_garrison_wsl_preflight`. Becomes a peer of the new linux/mac entries; otherwise we'd have one preflight in bujb and three in bujp, which defeats the seat.
- `zbujb_garrison_assert_platform` (the (letter, BURN_PLATFORM) compatibility gate) — arguably belongs as `zbujp_garrison_letter_platform_compat`. It's a precondition-with-diagnostic, same shape as the rest. Less obvious than the WSL one; defensible to leave in bujb if we want bujp narrowly scoped to "is the remote ready for the ceremony" probes.

I'd move the WSL one definitely; leave the platform-compat assertion in bujb for now (it's about argument shape, not remote state — different category).

**"Better module split overall?"** — Considering the candidates:

| Candidate split | Verdict |
|-----------------|---------|
| `bujp_preflight.sh` | **Earns it.** Three preflight entries, shared mechanism, clear concept boundary, future growth (fenestrate sshd-running probe, c-letter probe). |
| `bujt_transport.sh` (admin_exec, admin_powershell) | Doesn't earn it. Two helpers, used inline by both verbs, splitting creates a thin file. |
| `bujg_garrison.sh` / `bujf_fenestrate.sh` (verb-per-file) | Doesn't earn it. Fights the paddock's explicit "BCG-compliant module housing both verbs" decision. The verbs share enough infrastructure that co-location is the design. |
| `bujw_workload.sh` (knock, command_file, interactive_session) | Doesn't earn it yet. Three small helpers; coherent within bujb today. Reconsider if workload-side ops grow. |

So the only earned split is bujp. Everything else is fragmentation for fragmentation's sake.

**Net structure under this revision:**

`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`:
- kindle/sentinel
- shell-letter assertion, garrison platform-compat assertion
- admin_exec, admin_powershell (transport)
- garrison ceremony steps 1–6
- obliterate (windows-namespaces, workload)
- fenestrate ceremony (when added)
- workload-side: resolve, knock, command_file, interactive_session
- entry: `bujb_garrison`, `bujb_fenestrate`

`Tools/buk/bujp_preflight.sh` (new):
- kindle/sentinel
- `bujp_garrison_preflight` — dispatcher entry (called from garrison step 0)
- `zbujp_garrison_linux_preflight` — sudo probe
- `zbujp_garrison_mac_preflight` — admin group + sudo probe
- `zbujp_garrison_wsl_preflight` — WSL distro present probe (migrated from bujb)
- `zbujp_probe_sudo` — single-command non-interactive sudo probe
- `zbujp_expected_commands` — platform → command list
- `zbujp_render_snippet` — platform → sudoers fragment content
- `zbujp_stage_and_die` — scp + visudo -cf + buc_die
- `zbujp_admin_group_die` — Mac admin-group failure path

Acronym map gets one new line: `BUJP` → `Tools/buk/bujp_preflight.sh`.

Migration of the WSL preflight is in-scope for the same pace (or queued as a follow-on if we want to keep this pace mechanically tight).

Does this restructured shape sit better? If so, I'll capture the docket revision (in chat, no edits) reflecting the bujp module + per-platform entries.
Capturing the new docket via reslate. This touches only JJK state (`.claude/jjm/jjp_uAl-.md` via the redocket flow), not `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` — safe alongside the parallel chat.

New module mint plus per-platform preflight implementation.

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
8	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
9	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
10	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
11	
12	## Current Concept
13	
14	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
15	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
16	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
17	the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
18	BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
19	
20	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
21	
22	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
23	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
24	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
25	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
26	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BURC addition:**

- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

# jjezs_reslate ₢A-AAz

## Character
Design-locked; module mint plus mechanical mirroring across three platforms.

## Docket

Locked decisions:

- Mint `Tools/buk/bujp_preflight.sh` (acronym: BUJP) as the seat for all
  garrison preflights. Three platform entries (linux, mac, wsl) dispatched
  from garrison entry by `BURN_PLATFORM`.

- Migrate `zbujb_garrison_w_preflight` from bujb to bujp under the new
  per-platform naming axis.

- Sudo probe: `sudo -ln <command>` per-command, with the platform's expected
  command list derived from the existing `sudo -n` callsites in
  bujb_jurisdiction.sh.

- Sudoers posture: `/etc/sudoers.d/` drop-in (mode 0440, root-owned,
  dot-free filename). Reuse `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`.

- Failure recovery: render snippet locally, scp to remote home-cache (not
  /tmp), validate with `visudo -cf` over ssh, then `buc_die` with a
  copy-paste-safe `sudo install` line referencing the staged file. Probe
  before stage so the happy path makes no extra ssh round-trips.

- Mac admin-group probe via `dseditgroup -o checkmember -m <user> admin`.
  On failure, diagnostic names the `dseditgroup -o edit` fix path.

- BUS0 spec: paragraph under §Remote Node Access naming preflight as the
  precondition layer; BUJP entry in BUS0 mapping section.

- Paddock Standing Note: sudo NOPASSWD for `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` on
  linux/mac, and admin-group membership on mac, are operator-managed
  preconditions.

Done when:
- bash -n green on bujp_preflight.sh and bujb_jurisdiction.sh
- tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green
- Preflight diagnostics name their respective fix paths (visudo -cf for
  sudo, dseditgroup -o edit for mac admin group); verifiable by inspection
- BUJP entry added to acronym map in CLAUDE.md
- Paddock Standing Note added
